steps.title: Identify the appositive boundary
steps.content:
The text introduces “another instrument” and immediately needs to specify “the flute” as that instrument.

This is an appositive relationship requiring clear punctuation.

The key signal: after naming the flute, the text describes it as “relatively quiet” and why it mattered—a separate idea that needs its own sentence boundary.

steps.title: Apply the colon and period rule
steps.content:
A colon introduces an elaboration or appositive, making “music: the flute” the correct connection. A period after “flute” then marks a sentence boundary, allowing “A relatively quiet instrument” to start a new sentence where “its full range of sound was finally audible” is clearly the predicate. This removes ambiguity: is “The flute” or “its full range of sound” the subject? Choice D makes it unmistakably “A relatively quiet instrument” (with “its full range of sound” as an appositive elaboration).

The other choices fail: A creates a comma-separated list that incorrectly includes a predicate clause; B omits the period after flute, leaving the sentence structure ambiguous; C misuses a semicolon, since the second part isn't an independent clause.

That matches choice D — D is correct.
